message.content:
{
  "homework": {
    "sessionInformation": {
      "sessionNumber": "1",
      "sessionTitle": "Solutions - Session 1",
      "subject": "Chemistry",
      "grade": "Class XII / 12",
      "difficultyLevel": "Balanced",
      "learningPace": "Balanced",
      "estimatedHomeworkDuration": "45 minutes"
    },
    "homework": [
      {
        "id": 1,
        "type": "Conceptual Explanation & Definitions",
        "title": "Foundations of Solutions: Terminology and Classification",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_01_Define_Solutions"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Introduction to Solutions", "Types of Solutions", "Concentration Terms"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 5,
        "estimatedTime": "8 minutes",
        "instructions": "Define the following terms clearly and provide one real-world example for each. Classify the examples based on the state of matter (solid/liquid/gas) of both solute and solvent.",
        "question": "1. Define 'Solution', 'Solute', and 'Solvent'.\n2. List all 9 possible types of solutions with an example for each.\n3. Explain why a solution is considered a homogeneous mixture while a colloid or suspension is not, based on particle size.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Written response",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "Clear definitions of solute/solvent/solution. A complete table listing all 9 types (e.g., Gas in Liquid - Soda). Explanation distinguishing homogeneous mixtures from heterogeneous ones based on particle size (<1nm vs >1000nm)."
      },
      {
        "id": 2,
        "type": "Numerical Problem",
        "title": "Calculating Molarity and Molality of Solutions",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_02_Calculate_Concentration"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Molarity (M)", "Molality (m)"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 6,
        "estimatedTime": "12 minutes",
        "instructions": "Solve the following problems showing all steps. Use atomic masses: Na=23, Cl=35.5, O=16.",
        "question": "Problem A: Calculate the molarity of a solution prepared by dissolving 4g of NaOH in enough water to form 250 mL of solution.\n\nProblem B: Calculate the molality of a solution containing 18 g of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) dissolved in 250 g of water.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Show calculations",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "Problem A: Moles = 4/40 = 0.1 mol; Molarity = 0.1 / 0.25 L = 0.4 M.\n\nProblem B: Moles of glucose = 18/180 = 0.1 mol; Molality = 0.1 / 0.25 kg = 0.4 m."
      },
      {
        "id": 3,
        "type": "Comparative Analysis",
        "title": "Molarity vs. Molality: Temperature Dependence",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_03_Compare_Concentration"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Properties of Concentration Terms"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 4,
        "estimatedTime": "7 minutes",
        "instructions": "Explain the fundamental difference between Molarity and Molality. Why is molality preferred over molarity when dealing with temperature-dependent properties?",
        "question": "Compare Molarity (M) and Molality (m). Which one changes with a change in temperature? Justify your answer by referring to the definition of each term.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Short Answer",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "Explanation that Molarity depends on volume, which expands/contracts with temperature. Molality depends on mass (kg), which remains constant regardless of temperature changes."
      },
      {
        "id": 4,
        "type": "Real-World Application",
        "title": "Henry's Law in Daily Life",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_04_Apply_Gas_Laws"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Solubility of Gases in Liquids", "Factors Affecting Solubility"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 5,
        "estimatedTime": "10 minutes",
        "instructions": "Read the scenario below and answer the questions using your understanding of how temperature affects gas solubility.",
        "question": "Scenario: A diver ascends rapidly from deep water to the surface. Explain why this can lead to 'the bends' (decompression sickness) in terms of Henry's Law.\n\nAdditionally, explain why carbonated soft drinks are bottled under high pressure and stored at low temperatures.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Written explanation",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "Explanation that as external pressure decreases during ascent, the solubility of dissolved nitrogen gas in blood drops (Henry's Law), causing bubbles to form. Soft drinks are bottled under high pressure to increase CO2 solubility and kept cold because gas solubility increases at lower temperatures."
      },
      {
        "id": 5,
        "type": "Diagram Labelling",
        "title": "Visualizing Ideal vs Non-Ideal Solutions",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_05_Identify_Solution_Behavior"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Ideal and Non-Ideal Solutions"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 4,
        "estimatedTime": "8 minutes",
        "instructions": "Draw a simple graph of Partial Vapor Pressure vs. Mole Fraction for an Ideal Solution (Raoult's Law). Label the axes and indicate whether the line is linear or curved.",
        "question": "Sketch the vapor pressure curve for an ideal solution obeying Raoult's law.\n\nThen, describe two conditions under which a real solution would show positive deviation from this ideal behavior. What does 'positive deviation' imply about intermolecular forces?",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Draw and Write",
        "visualRequirement": "Graph of Partial Vapor Pressure vs Mole Fraction showing a straight line.",
        "expectedResponse": "A linear graph connecting the vapor pressures of pure components. Conditions for positive deviation: Weaker A-B interactions than A-A or B-B; Endothermic mixing (ΔH_mix > 0)."
      },
      {
        "id": 6,
        "type": "Home Experiment / Observation",
        "title": "Observing Solubility and Saturation at Home",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["LO_06_Observe_Saturation"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Saturated vs Unsaturated Solutions"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 3,
        "estimatedTime": "15 minutes (including observation time)",
        "instructions": "Perform a safe home experiment to observe saturation.",
        "question": "Take two glasses of water at room temperature. Add table salt (NaCl) gradually to the first glass until no more dissolves and crystals remain at the bottom. Note this as 'Saturated'.\n\nNow, take the second glass with half a cup of water. Heat it gently on a stove or kettle (with adult supervision). Try adding salt again.\n\nObservation Question: Did you observe that hot water can dissolve more salt than cold water? Write down your observation and relate it to the general trend for solid solutes in liquid solvents.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Observation Log",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "Student observes that heating allows more salt to dissolve. Conclusion: Solubility of most solids increases with temperature."
      }
    ],
    "summary": {
      "totalQuestions": 6,
      "totalMarks": 27,
      "estimatedCompletionTime": "45 minutes",
      "learningOutcomesCovered": [
        "LO_01_Define_Solutions",
        "LO_02_Calculate_Concentration",
        "LO_03_Compare_Concentration",
        "LO_04_Apply_Gas_Laws",
        "LO_05_Identify_Solution_Behavior",
        "LO_06_Observe_Saturation"
      ],
      "topicsCovered": [
        "Introduction to Solutions",
        "Types of Solutions",
        "Concentration Terms (Molarity, Molality)",
        "Solubility and Henry's Law",
        "Ideal vs Non-Ideal Solutions"
      ],
      "subtopicsCovered": [
        "Terminology (Solute/Solvent)",
        "9 Types of Mixtures",
        "Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous",
        "Temperature dependence of concentration terms",
        "Gas solubility factors",
        "Raoult's Law deviations"
      ],
      "taskDistribution": {
        "Conceptual Explanation & Definitions": 1,
        "Numerical Problem": 2,
        "Comparative Analysis": 1,
        "Real-World Application": 1,
        "Diagram Labelling": 1,
        "Home Experiment / Observation": 1
      },
      "homeExperimentIncluded": true,
      "parentEngagementIncluded": false
    }
  }
}
